content.text: Incident Commander Role and Roster Management

During a high-severity Claude incident, many engineers work in parallel on different parts of the problem. Without a single coordinator, efforts duplicate, decisions conflict, and customer communication gets delayed. The Incident Commander (IC) is the function that prevents that failure mode.

The IC does not fix the incident — the technical lead and subject matter experts do. The IC owns the process: classification, role assignment, decision authority, external communication, and ultimately the call to declare resolution. Separating coordination from implementation is the insight that makes large-scale incidents manageable.

attrs.alt: IC org chart and rotation cycle diagram

content.text: These responsibilities apply from the moment the IC is paged until the incident is declared resolved and handed to the post-incident review owner.
Classify and set severity within 5 minutes.
Run the classification decision tree (or review the auto-classification). Set the severity in the incident ticket. If the auto-classifier produced P4 but customer reports say otherwise, override it.
Open the war-room bridge and assign roles.
For P0/P1, open a dedicated Slack channel and a video bridge. Explicitly assign the tech lead, comms lead, scribe, and any needed SMEs. Unassigned roles lead to coverage gaps.
The IC asks questions, removes blockers, and makes decisions. They do not write code or push configuration changes. An IC who starts debugging has lost the coordination thread.
No status page update or customer notification goes out without IC approval. This prevents contradictory messages from multiple people.

The IC calls the incident resolved when: the root cause is understood or contained, customer impact has stopped, and monitoring confirms recovery. They hand off to the post-incident review (PIR) owner at this point.
Update severity if new evidence arrives.
If investigation reveals a safety or privacy dimension that was not visible at classification time, the IC upgrades severity and triggers the appropriate escalation chain immediately.
What the IC Does NOT Own
Clarity on boundaries prevents the IC from becoming a bottleneck.
Drafting the status page update text
Deciding whether a deploy caused it
For P0 and P1 incidents, the IC immediately assembles a minimal team with explicit roles. Every person in the bridge should know their role.
Coordination, decisions, severity authority, resolution call
Status page updates, customer notifications, internal stakeholder brief
Real-time timeline log, action items, decisions record
Domain expertise: model, safety, infra, data, legal (as needed)
Subject-matter on-call or pulled from roster
Decision authority for customer credits, SLA breach, public disclosure
For P2 incidents, only the IC and tech lead roles are required. Comms lead and scribe are optional. SMEs are pulled in only if the tech lead needs them.

Not everyone on the SRE team should be IC-eligible from day one. An unqualified IC who is uncertain about their authority slows the incident down. A formal qualification process ensures every IC on the roster can act confidently.
At least 6 months on the SRE or platform engineering team
Completed the internal incident management training course
Shadowed at least three P0 or P1 incidents as a scribe or SME
Passed the IC certification exercise (tabletop simulation with a P0 scenario)

The tabletop simulation runs for 90 minutes. A facilitator walks the candidate through a realistic P0 scenario — model serving outage that evolves into a safety incident midway through. The candidate is evaluated on:
Correct type and severity within 5 minutes of brief
All four core roles filled within 10 minutes
Upgrades to P0 within 2 minutes of safety signal appearing
Status update authorised within 15 minutes of P0 declaration
Correctly identifies all three resolution criteria before declaring
PIR owner named and briefed before leaving bridge
A healthy IC roster has enough depth to cover continuous on-call without burning out individuals and enough breadth to handle simultaneous incidents.
Fewer than 8 forces back-to-back rotations, leading to fatigue incidents
Shorter rotations increase handoff overhead; longer ones increase fatigue
Candidates observe live incidents with no decision responsibility
Prevents burnout; each IC is primary roughly once per 4 weeks
1 primary IC per incident; escalate for simultaneous P0s
An IC managing two simultaneous P0s will drop coordination on at least one

Manage the IC rotation in PagerDuty (or equivalent) as a dedicated schedule separate from the SRE on-call. Keeping them separate lets you assign a different person to IC duties than the one debugging the system.

# Minimal PagerDuty rotation schedule via API
# Uses the PagerDuty v2 REST API

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

PD_API_KEY = "YOUR_PAGERDUTY_API_KEY"
SCHEDULE_ID = "P1234AB"   # IC rotation schedule ID

def get_current_ic() -> dict:
    """Return the on-call IC for the current moment."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://api.pagerduty.com/schedules/{SCHEDULE_ID}/users",
        params={"since": now, "until": now},
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Token token={PD_API_KEY}",
            "Accept": "application/vnd.pagerduty+json;version=2",
        },
        timeout=5,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    users = resp.json().get("users", [])
    return users[0] if users else {}

def get_upcoming_ics(days: int = 14) -> list[dict]:
    """Return IC coverage for the next N days."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    until = now + timedelta(days=days)
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://api.pagerduty.com/schedules/{SCHEDULE_ID}",
        params={"since": now.isoformat(), "until": until.isoformat()},
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Token token={PD_API_KEY}",
            "Accept": "application/vnd.pagerduty+json;version=2",
        },
        timeout=5,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["schedule"]["final_schedule"]["rendered_schedule_entries"]
When the IC rotation changes during an active incident — or when the IC needs a break during a multi-hour P0 — a formal handoff prevents information loss.
Outgoing IC briefs incoming IC in a 1:1 voice call (not Slack messages)
Incoming IC confirms understanding of: current severity, incident type, active mitigation steps, open decisions, and next communication deadline.
Outgoing IC updates the incident ticket with handoff time and incoming IC name
Incoming IC posts to the war-room bridge: "[IC handoff] Alice -> Bob, current status: [one-line summary]"
Outgoing IC stays available for 15 minutes after handoff for questions

A handoff that happens as a Slack message with no voice call is a high-risk handoff. Critical context — the IC's intuition about what is about to change or the subdued disagreement in the bridge — does not transfer over text.
Collect these metrics per-IC and per-incident to improve the roster and the training program.
IC familiarity with the tree; automation gaps
Comms lead effectiveness; IC approval latency
IC burnout proxy (PTO taken after rotation)
Tooling: Slack Bot Commands for IC Actions
Automate the mechanical parts of IC duties so the commander can focus on judgment rather than form-filling. A simple Slack bot handles bridge creation, role tracking, and status update templating.

# ic_bot.py — FastAPI Slack slash-command handler
from fastapi import FastAPI, Form
from typing import Annotated
import httpx, os

app = FastAPI()
SLACK_TOKEN = os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"]

async def slack_post(channel: str, text: str) -> None:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(
            "https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage",
            json={"channel": channel, "text": text},
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SLACK_TOKEN}"},
        )

@app.post("/ic/declare")
async def declare_incident(
    text: Annotated[str, Form()],      # "P1 availability/partial-degradation"
    user_name: Annotated[str, Form()],
):
    parts = text.strip().split(None, 1)
    severity, inc_type = parts[0], parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 else "unclassified"
    channel = f"incident-{severity.lower()}-{inc_type.replace('/', '-')}"

    # Create the channel (idempotent)
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(
            "https://slack.com/api/conversations.create",
            json={"name": channel, "is_private": False},
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SLACK_TOKEN}"},
        )

    await slack_post(channel, (
        f":rotating_light: *{severity} Incident Declared* :rotating_light:\n"
        f"Type: `{inc_type}`\n"
        f"IC: @{user_name}\n"
        f"Status: *investigating*\n"
        f"Next update in 15 minutes.\n\n"
        f"_Role assignments needed: Tech Lead, Comms Lead, Scribe_"
    ))
    return {"response_type": "in_channel", "text": f"Bridge opened: #{channel}"}

@app.post("/ic/assign")
async def assign_role(text: Annotated[str, Form()], channel_name: Annotated[str, Form()]):
    # text: "tech-lead @alice" or "comms @bob"
    role, user = text.strip().split(None, 1)
    await slack_post(f"#{channel_name}", f":white_check_mark: *{role}* assigned to {user}")
    return {"response_type": "in_channel", "text": f"Assigned {role} to {user}"}
Two simultaneous P0s are the hardest scenario for the IC function. The primary IC cannot split their attention between two separate bridges without losing coordination quality on both.
The primary IC takes the first P0. They do not attempt to manage both.
The shadow IC immediately activates as full IC for the second incident. If the shadow is not yet certified, the roster manager pages the next qualified IC in the on-call list.
Both ICs establish separate Slack bridges. They cross-post a brief awareness message — "Aware of simultaneous P0 on infra; not managing it" — so engineers in each bridge know the other exists.
If both incidents share a root cause (e.g. a single bad deploy caused two separate failure modes), the two ICs agree on one lead IC who makes the resolution call for both.
Never try to manage two simultaneous P0s from a single Slack bridge. Engineers from both incidents posting to the same channel creates confusion about which actions apply to which incident.
Qualification is a gate, not the end of training. IC skills decay without practice, and new incident types (safety incidents, novel infra failures) require ongoing learning.
Stay current with new incident types without primary responsibility
Learn from recent failures; understand how the classification evolved
Simulated production failures with full IC, tech lead, comms, and scribe roles
IC-specific training on safety escalation paths and compliance obligations

A gameday runs for 2-3 hours. A facilitator injects faults into a staging environment that mirrors production. The IC team responds as they would in a real incident. Debrief identifies gaps in tooling, runbooks, and IC decisions.
Inject a single fault initially (e.g. model serving replica count drops)
Midway through, reveal a second dimension (e.g. the replica failure was caused by a bad config that also affected auth)
At the 90-minute mark, inject a safety signal — guardrail probe failure rate spikes
Evaluate: did the IC upgrade to P0 on the safety signal? Did they re-run the classification tree?
Debrief with all participants; file actionable items against runbooks and tooling
Automate roster health checks so the engineering manager knows before a coverage gap occurs rather than discovering it during an incident.

from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RosterHealth:
    qualified_ic_count: int
    primary_covered_days: int       # days with a qualified primary IC scheduled
    shadow_covered_days: int
    gaps: list[str]                 # ISO date strings with no coverage

def check_roster_health(upcoming_entries: list[dict], days: int = 28) -> RosterHealth:
    """
    upcoming_entries: PagerDuty rendered_schedule_entries
    Each entry has: start, end, user.summary
    """
    qualified = set()
    primary_days = 0
    shadow_days = 0
    gaps = []
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

    for offset in range(days):
        day = now + timedelta(days=offset)
        day_str = day.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        covered = any(
            entry["start"] <= day.isoformat() <= entry["end"]
            for entry in upcoming_entries
        )
        if covered:
            primary_days += 1
        else:
            gaps.append(day_str)
        for entry in upcoming_entries:
            qualified.add(entry["user"]["summary"])

    return RosterHealth(
        qualified_ic_count=len(qualified),
        primary_covered_days=primary_days,
        shadow_covered_days=shadow_days,
        gaps=gaps,
    )

def alert_if_unhealthy(health: RosterHealth) -> list[str]:
    alerts = []
    if health.qualified_ic_count < 4:
        alerts.append(f"CRITICAL: Only {health.qualified_ic_count} qualified ICs — minimum is 4")
    if health.gaps:
        alerts.append(f"Coverage gaps in {len(health.gaps)} days: {health.gaps[:3]}...")
    return alerts
Once an IC opens a terminal and starts running queries, the war-room loses its coordinator. The IC should ask the tech lead to pull the data and share findings in the bridge.
Without a named comms lead, status page updates get written by whoever has a moment — leading to inconsistent messages and missed deadlines.
Rotating IC mid-incident without a formal handoff.
A Slack message saying "I'm going to bed, you've got it" is not a handoff. Context loss at handoff is a leading cause of incidents extending past their natural resolution point.
IC roster with fewer than 4 qualified engineers.
Below four, any illness or overlap forces back-to-back rotations. Expand the qualification pipeline before the roster shrinks below this threshold.
IC who has never shadowed a safety incident.
Safety incidents require a different escalation path. Ensure each IC candidate has observed at least one safety or privacy incident before certification.

The Incident Commander role provides a single coordination function that keeps engineers focused on the technical problem while ensuring classification, communication, and decision-making happen correctly and on time. The IC does not fix the incident — they create the conditions for others to fix it quickly.
IC owns: classification, role assignment, comms approval, severity updates, resolution declaration
IC does not own: the fix, status page text, post-incident analysis, regulatory notification
Qualification requires shadowing three incidents and passing a tabletop simulation
Minimum roster: 8 qualified ICs on a 4-week cycle (1 week primary, 1 week shadow, 2 weeks off)
Handoffs during active incidents require a voice call — Slack messages lose critical context
Track time-to-classification and severity-upgrade-delay as leading quality indicators for IC performance
